items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.correct: They have a full outer electron shell
items.why: Correct answer: They have a full outer electron shell. “They are too heavy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which noble gas is famously used to fill balloons?
items.why: Correct answer: Helium. “Argon” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the group number of the noble gases?
items.why: The real figure is 0. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
